schools.summary: The opening of the heavenly vision — 'after this' (meta tauta, a change in the panorama of visions, not strict chronology, Robertson; the letters giving way to a heavenly scene); 'a door was opened in heaven' (an aperture granting sight of the celestial throne-room); the summoning voice like a trumpet, 'Come up hither' (calling the seer up to heaven's vantage); 'I will shew thee things which must be hereafter' (God's ordained future unveiled, the rest of the book flowing hence); history unveiled only after its Sovereign is seen.
Victorinus: the open door in heaven announces the new testament's access to heaven.

themes: a door was opened in heaven

schools.summary: The central throne and its Occupant — 'immediately I was in the spirit' (caught into the prophetic ecstasy, as Rev 1:10, perhaps a higher degree, the soul absorbed in the heavenly sight); 'a throne was set in heaven' (the first thing seen, fixed and established, the seat of universal sovereignty, all else arranged around it, the governing symbol of the book); 'one sat on the throne' (God himself, unnamed and undescribed in form, the reticence guarding the divine majesty); the future unveiled only after God's sovereignty is established.

themes: a throne was set in heaven

schools.summary: The glory of the enthroned God — no form or shape given, only radiant splendour, lest a likeness be made (Clarke); 'like a jasper and a sardine stone' (the jasper brilliant and light-flashing, Rev 21:11, the sardine fiery red; imaging blended glory, some suggesting holiness and justice, purity and consuming fire; like the high priest's breastplate jewels, Wesley); 'a rainbow round about the throne, like an emerald' (a complete circle of soothing green, the ancient sign of God's covenant and mercy, the throne of majesty ringed with the token of grace, mercy softening majesty).

themes: like a jasper and a sardine stone
a rainbow round about the throne

schools.summary: The four and twenty elders — 'round about the throne... four and twenty seats/thrones' (lesser thrones encircling the great one); commonly the redeemed church glorified — twenty-four as the twelve patriarchs and twelve apostles, old and new covenant saints together, or the twenty-four priestly courses (Clarke); their royal-priestly dignity 'clothed in white raiment' (purity and victory) and 'crowns of gold' (those who have overcome and reign); the saints pictured nearest the throne, enthroned and crowned, before the angels are mentioned, marking the redeemed's high honour.

themes: white raiment and crowns of gold

schools.summary: The throne's majesty and the sevenfold Spirit — 'lightnings and thunderings and voices' (the signs of Sinai, Ex 19:16; Ezek 1:13, expressing terrible majesty, power, and holiness; lightnings striking the sight, voices the hearing, thunders the body, Wesley) proceeding 'out of the throne' (God's power and judgments from where he reigns); 'seven lamps of fire... which are the seven Spirits of God' (the Holy Spirit in the fulness and perfection of his operations, 'seven' denoting completeness, burning before the throne as light and fire).

The crystal sea and the living creatures — 'a sea of glass like unto crystal' (a wide, calm, transparent expanse, clear and still, an emblem of God's pure peace and holiness, or the cleansing by which the redeemed approach); the rendering 'beasts' corrected — the word means 'living creatures' (zoa), not brute beasts (Clarke); the four, drawn from Ezekiel and Isaiah, in closest attendance on God; 'full of eyes before and behind' (perfect knowledge, ceaseless vigilance, all-seeing intelligence); their identity variously taken — angelic ministers, cherubim, or creation in God's service.

themes: a sea of glass like unto crystal
full of eyes before and behind

schools.summary: The four faces of the living creatures — 'like a lion' (strength and majesty), 'like a calf/ox' (patient labour and sacrifice), 'a face as a man' (reason and wisdom), 'like a flying eagle' (swiftness, soaring height, keen sight); representing whatever is noblest, strongest, wisest, and swiftest in animate creation (Swete), the crown of created life before the throne; connected with Ezekiel's cherubim (Ezek 1:10, each with all four faces) and the four tribal standards of Israel; the church also seeing the four Evangelists, though the text does not assign them.

debates.question: What do the four living creatures (lion, ox, man, eagle) represent?

sides.position: the noblest, strongest, wisest, and swiftest of animate creation, the crown of created life worshipping God (cf. Ezekiel's cherubim and the four tribal standards)
emblems of the four Evangelists (the church's traditional reading, not assigned by the text)

schools.summary: The unceasing song of the living creatures — 'six wings' (as Isaiah's seraphim, Isa 6:2; covering the face in reverence, the feet in humility, flying in service, Wesley) and 'full of eyes within' (all knowledge and vigilance); 'they rest not day and night' (worship the unbroken occupation of heaven); the song 'Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty' (Isaiah's anthem, the threefold ascription stressing God's infinite transcendent purity, the church hearing a hint of the Trinity); 'which was, and is, and is to come' (the eternal, unchanging One).

themes: they rest not day and night
Lord God Almighty, which was, is, and is to come

schools.summary: The creatures leading the worship — 'when those beasts give glory and honour and thanks' (repeated, recurring action; as often as the four living creatures lift their praise, the elders' response follows); the threefold tribute — glory due his majesty, honour due his name, thanks for his goodness; 'him that sat on the throne, who liveth for ever and ever' (the eternal God, his endless being the ground of endless praise); heaven's worship in ordered antiphon — the creatures leading, the redeemed elders responding, the constant occupation of heaven.

themes: give glory and honour and thanks
him that sat on the throne
who liveth for ever and ever

schools.summary: The elders' homage — 'fall down before him that sat on the throne' (the glorified saints, themselves crowned and enthroned, casting themselves down in adoration, the highest of the redeemed bowing lowest); 'worship him that liveth for ever and ever' (adoring the eternal God); 'cast their crowns before the throne' (laying their golden crowns at God's feet, as subject kings before a sovereign in submission, Clarke; confessing that all their honour and reward come from God alone, rendering back what he gave, claiming no glory); the humility of heaven, the perfection of worship.

themes: worship him that liveth for ever
cast their crowns before the throne

schools.summary: The song of the Creator's worthiness — 'thou art worthy, O Lord' (best texts 'our Lord and our God,' cf. John 20:28) 'to receive glory and honour and power' (the threefold tribute owned as God's due); the ground 'for thou hast created all things' (worthiness resting on creation, the Maker of all deserving the homage of all); 'for thy pleasure they are and were created' (all things owing original creation and continued existence to God's sovereign will); creation from, by, and for God; opening heaven's worship before the song of redemption follows.

themes: for thy pleasure they were created
